perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS the TTC is issuing a Request for Pr oposals for the Provision of Armoured Car Services For a Five Year Term, R equest for Proposal No. P27CB10251 (“RFP”), and the Company is interested on receiving a copy of the RFP in order to determine whether to submit a bid bas ed on the RFP (“Purpose”).
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS the TTC is issuing a Request for Pr oposals for the Provision of Armoured Car Services For a Five Year Term, R equest for Proposal No. P27CB10251 (“RFP”).
perturbation.explanation: By removing the part that reads 'and the Company is interested on receiving a copy of the RFP in order to determine whether to submit a bid bas ed on the RFP (“Purpose”).' , the connection between the TTC issuing the RFP and the Company's interest in it is severed. This affects the stated 'Purpose' of the NDA, making the rest of the agreement contextually conflicting, as the 'Purpose' isn't explicit anymore
perturbation.contradicted_location: CLAUSE 2. USE AND DISCLOSURE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION
perturbation.contradicted_text: CLAUSE 2. USE AND DISCLOSURE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION 2.1 The Company shall: 2.1.1 Use the Confidential Information sole ly for the Purpose as set out above; 2.1.2 Hold the Conf idential Information in confiden ce and shall not sell, assign, transfer or otherwise disclose the Confiden tial Information, or any information or materials derived there from, to any thir d party without the prior consent of the TTC, save and except as otherwise provided herein; 2.1.3 Employ at least the same degree of care to protect the secrecy and confidentiality of the Conf idential Information as it uses to protect its own confidential and proprietary information and materials, but in no event less than reasonable care; and 2.1.4 Restrict the release, access and us e of the Confidenti al Information to those employees and officers who must have access to the Confidential Information in order to pe rmit the Company, in good fa ith, to determine whether to submit a bid based on the RFP, sa ve and except as otherwise provided herein; 2.1.5 Ensure that each person to whom C onfidential Information is disclosed to in accordance with Clause 2.1.4 is advis ed, prior to the di sclosure, of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly stated the 'Purpose' of the NDA, linking the TTC's RFP issuance to the Company's interest in potentially submitting a bid. The changed text removes this explicit 'Purpose'. The 'Contradicted Text' refers to and relies on the 'Purpose' defined earlier. Because the 'Purpose' has been removed by the change, the contradicted section is now in direct conflict with the modified text, as it references something that no longer exists, causing an in-text contradiction. Therefore, the answer is YES.

perturbation.original_text: 2.3 Immediately upon written request by the TTC and subject to any statutory requirement, the Company shall return all copies of the Confid ential Information in its possession to the requesting party or certify that all copies in its possession or control have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.3 Immediately upon written request by the TTC, the Company shall return all copies of the Information in its possession to the requesting party or certify that all copies in its possession or control have been destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the term 'Confidential Information' to 'Information', the scope of what needs to be returned or destroyed is broadened. If the TTC provides the Company with general (non-confidential) information alongside specific confidential details, the clause now mandates the return/destruction of *all* information, creating an obligation beyond the original intent of the NDA. This can lead to conflict and becomes uncertain and broad, contradicting the underlying purpose of protecting only confidential items.
perturbation.location: CLAUSE 2. USE AND DISCLOSURE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1 In this Agreement, “Confidential Information” means any information designated as confidential by TTC, whether disclosed in writing, orally, or through any other means.
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the scope of 'Confidential Information' to simply 'Information'. The contradicted text defines 'Confidential Information' as specifically designated by TTC. By removing 'Confidential', the changed text now requires the Company to return/destroy *all* information, not just the information TTC designated as confidential, as originally agreed. This creates a direct contradiction and a broader obligation, as any information given by TTC now has to be returned, even if not confidential.

perturbation.original_text: 2.4 In the event of a breach of any of the foregoing provisions, TTC and the Company agree that the harm suffered by the TTC would not be compensable by monetary damages alone and accordingly, that the TTC shall, in addition to other available legal or equitable remedies, be entitled to an injunction against such NO N-DISCLOSURE / CONFIDENTIALITY AGREEMENT breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.4 In the event of a breach of any of the foregoing provisions, TTC and the Company agree that the harm suffered by the TTC would not be compensable by monetary damages alone.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause that gives the TTC the right to seek an injunction against breaches, the remedy available to the TTC in case of a breach is significantly limited. This now implies the harm suffered is now only compensable with monetary damages contradicting the previous assertion. This reduces the enforceability of the NDA and contradicts the assumption that confidentiality is paramount and requires more than just monetary compensation.
perturbation.location: CLAUSE 2. USE AND DISCLOSURE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION
perturbation.contradicted_location: CLAUSE 2. USE AND DISCLOSURE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 The Company shall: 2.1.1 Use the Confidential Information sole ly for the Purpose as set out above; 2.1.2 Hold the Conf idential Information in confiden ce and shall not sell, assign, transfer or otherwise disclose the Confiden tial Information, or any information or materials derived there from, to any thir d party without the prior consent of the TTC, save and except as otherwise provided herein; 2.1.3 Employ at least the same degree of care to protect the secrecy and confidentiality of the Conf idential Information as it uses to protect its own confidential and proprietary information and materials, but in no event less than reasonable care; and 2.1.4 Restrict the release, access and us e of the Confidenti al Information to those employees and officers who must have access to the Confidential Information in order to pe rmit the Company, in good fa ith, to determine whether to submit a bid based on the RFP, sa ve and except as otherwise provided herein; 2.1.5 Ensure that each person to whom C onfidential Information is disclosed to in accordance with Clause 2.1.4 is advis ed, prior to the di sclosure, of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text in 2.4 stated that in case of a breach, TTC is entitled to an injunction. The changed text removes the right to an injunction, limiting TTC's remedies to monetary damages only. This contradicts section 2.1, which outlines the company's obligation to protect confidential information. The original text ensures this obligation is strongly enforced through potential injunctions. By removing the injunction option, the changed text weakens the protection of confidential information outlined in 2.1, implying monetary damages are sufficient which directly contradicts the intention of 2.1 requiring more robust protection through confidentiality obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Notwithstanding anything to the contra ry herein, the Company’s confidentiality obligations shall not apply to Confidential Information that: 3.1.1 Is known to the Company at the time of disclosure; 3.1.2 Is independently developed by the Co mpany provided that party can show that such development was accomp lished without the use of or any reference to the Confidential Information; 3.1.3 Becomes known to the Com pany from another source without confidentiality restriction on subsequent disclosure or use, provided that such source is not in breach of its obligations of non-disclosure; 3.1.4 Is or becomes a par t of the public domain thr ough no wrongful act of the Company; or 3.1.5 Is subsequently publicly disclosed by the TTC.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Notwithstanding anything to the contra ry herein, the Company’s obligations shall not apply to Information that: 3.1.1 Is known to the Company at the time of disclosure;
perturbation.explanation: The original clause outlines specific exceptions to the confidentiality obligations, providing a balanced approach to the NDA. The modified version drastically shortens this clause, only providing one, and broad exception. It removes the exceptions for independently developed information, information from other sources without confidentiality restrictions, information that becomes public domain, or information publicly disclosed by the TTC. This creates a very unbalanced NDA and the Company is much more liable. These omissions create an internal contradiction, because the company can no longer operate without breaking the agreement
perturbation.contradicted_text: CLAUSE 5. NO IMPLIED OBLIGATIONS 5.1 Unless a written agr eement with respect to the Purpose is entered into by the Parties (and except for the matters spec ifically agreed to herein), neither party shall be under any legal obligation with re spect to the Purpose or otherwise by virtue of this Agreement or any written or oral expression with respect to the Purpose by any of their respective dire ctors, officers, employees or agents.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the exceptions to the Company's confidentiality obligations. The original text provided multiple exceptions. By removing those exceptions, the new version creates a situation where the Company is obligated to maintain confidentiality even in scenarios where it would not have been under the original agreement. This contradicts Clause 5, which states that neither party shall be under any legal obligation with respect to the Purpose except for the matters specifically agreed to. With fewer exceptions in 3.1, the Company is now under broader obligations, thus contradicting the 'no implied obligations' clause.
